title: JSON Collections & Document API
description: Work with Oracle as a pure document database. Insert, query, transform, search, and aggregate JSON documents — all with SQL.
introduction:
In this module, you're going to use Oracle as a pure document database. No tables. No schema. No DDL for the documents themselves. You'll work with JSON Collection Tables — a storage model where each row holds a flexible JSON document, and the database imposes zero structural requirements on what goes inside. If you've used MongoDB, this will feel familiar. If you've only used relational databases, prepare to have your assumptions challenged about what Oracle can do.

The critical technology underneath is OSON — Oracle's binary JSON format. When you insert a JSON document, Oracle converts it from text to OSON on write, storing it with hash-indexed field navigation that provides O(1) field access.

[svg:m1-oson-storage.svg]

This is a fundamental physics advantage over MongoDB's BSON format, which uses length-prefixed sequential traversal — meaning every field access requires scanning from the beginning of the document. At field position 50, OSON is 28.6x faster. At position 1000, it's 529x faster. Every query you run in this module benefits from that binary format advantage.

You'll use dot notation with type-safe accessors (.string(), .number(), .boolean()) to extract fields without defining a schema upfront. You'll use JSON_TRANSFORM to perform surgical, server-side document updates — SET a field, APPEND to an array, REMOVE a key — without the read-modify-write anti-pattern that plagues application-level document manipulation. You'll create a JSON Search Index that provides full-text search with transactional consistency, meaning your search results reflect the committed state of the data at query time. Compare that to MongoDB Atlas Search, which runs on a separate Lucene-based sync process with 1 to 15 seconds of eventual consistency lag — a window where your search results can contradict your document reads.

By the end of this module, you'll have inserted documents, queried them with dot notation, transformed them in place, searched them with full-text indexing, and aggregated them with SQL GROUP BY and AVG — all against schemaless JSON collections. The punchline: Oracle's Cost-Based Optimizer, with four decades of query optimization research, plans and executes these operations exactly like relational queries. You get document flexibility with relational performance. That's not a tradeoff. That's convergence.
checkpoint: You just used Oracle as a document database — no tables, no schema, no DDL for the documents themselves. But you also ran SQL aggregations over those documents. That's convergence.

exercises.title: Explore the Collection
exercises.description: View documents in the interactions collection and count them.
exercises.explanation:
When Oracle stores a JSON document, it doesn't store the text you gave it. It converts that text into **OSON** — Oracle's binary JSON format — a hash-indexed representation where every field name is mapped to an offset table. This means the database can jump directly to any field in the document in **O(1) time**, regardless of document size or field position. But OSON is a machine-optimized format. To see your document as a human, you need **JSON_SERIALIZE** — the function that converts OSON binary back into UTF-8 JSON text for display.

[svg:m1-json-serialize.svg]

The **PRETTY** keyword instructs JSON_SERIALIZE to emit indented, line-broken output. Without it, you get a single dense line — technically valid JSON, but nearly impossible to scan when documents contain nested objects and arrays. This is not cosmetic. When you're debugging a document with 30 fields and 3 levels of nesting, PRETTY is the difference between finding the problem in seconds and missing it entirely. The **ORDERED** option (available in 23ai and later) sorts object keys alphabetically, which is invaluable when you need to compare two documents side by side — without it, field order is implementation-defined and may vary between documents.

The **ASCII** option escapes all non-ASCII characters to `\uXXXX` sequences, which matters when your downstream consumer doesn't handle UTF-8 correctly — legacy systems, certain message queues, or CSV exports. These three options — PRETTY, ORDERED, ASCII — compose freely in any combination.

**FETCH FIRST N ROWS ONLY** is the SQL standard row-limiting clause, and it's essential discipline when exploring an unfamiliar collection. Without it, you might pull thousands of documents into your result set and overwhelm the client. **COUNT(*)** works on JSON Collection Tables exactly as it does on relational tables — the optimizer reads collection metadata to return the count efficiently, without scanning every document. This is a direct consequence of Oracle's unified storage engine: JSON collections are tables, so every SQL operation that works on tables works on collections. There's no translation layer. There's no adapter.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY ORDERED) FROM client_interactions FETCH FIRST 1 ROWS ONLY` — keys sorted alphabetically for easier scanning
- `SELECT json_serialize(data) FROM client_interactions FETCH FIRST 1 ROWS ONLY` — compare the compact single-line form to see why PRETTY matters

steps.label: View the first 3 documents with pretty formatting
steps.code:
SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY) FROM client_interactions
FETCH FIRST 3 ROWS ONLY;

steps.label: Count total documents in the collection
steps.code: SELECT COUNT(*) AS doc_count FROM client_interactions;

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM client_interactions
exercises.checkpoint: You just queried JSON documents with SQL and formatted them with JSON_SERIALIZE. Oracle stored those documents in OSON binary format — hash-indexed, O(1) field access. No schema definition required.

exercises.title: Query by Example
exercises.description: Find meetings with follow-up required and interactions for a specific client using dot notation.
exercises.explanation:
Oracle's **simplified dot notation** is the bridge between schemaless documents and strongly-typed SQL. When you write `ci.data.type.string()`, you're telling the database three things: navigate into the `data` column of alias `ci`, follow the path to field `type`, and return the value as a SQL `VARCHAR2`. The type accessor — **.string()**, **.number()**, **.boolean()**, **.date()**, **.timestamp()** — is what makes this safe. It's not just a cast; it instructs the OSON binary navigator to extract the field value and convert it to a specific SQL type with proper comparison semantics. String equality, numeric ranges, boolean logic — all work correctly because the type is declared at extraction time, not guessed.

[svg:m1-dot-notation.svg]

This is fundamentally different from how MongoDB handles queries. In MongoDB, field access is untyped — you specify `{type: "meeting"}` and the engine figures out the comparison. That works until your collection contains documents where `type` is sometimes a string and sometimes a number, and suddenly your query silently skips documents that don't match the implied type. Oracle's explicit type accessors make this impossible: if a field contains a number but you call `.string()`, you get a defined conversion, not a silent mismatch.

If a document lacks the specified path entirely, the expression returns **NULL** rather than throwing an error. This makes dot notation safe for heterogeneous collections where not every document has the same shape — a common reality in event logs, interaction records, and any collection that evolves over time. You can filter on fields that only some documents possess without worrying about runtime exceptions.

For paths deeper than one level, you chain through nested objects: `ci.data.address.city.string()`. For string values that might exceed 4000 bytes, use **.clob()** instead of `.string()` to avoid truncation. The optimizer can leverage **functional indexes** on dot notation expressions — meaning `CREATE INDEX idx ON tbl(data.type.string())` gives you B-tree performance on a schemaless field, exactly like indexing a relational column.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT ci.data.type.string() AS type, ci.data.sentiment.string() AS sentiment, ci.data.summary.string() AS summary FROM client_interactions ci FETCH FIRST 5 ROWS ONLY` — extract multiple fields into a relational result set
- `SELECT DISTINCT ci.data.type.string() AS interaction_type FROM client_interactions ci` — discover all distinct interaction types in the collection
exercises.code:
-- Find all meetings with follow-up required
SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY)
FROM client_interactions ci
WHERE ci.data.type.string() = 'meeting'
  AND ci.data.follow_up_required.boolean() = TRUE;

-- Find interactions for a specific client
SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY)
FROM client_interactions ci
WHERE ci.data.client_id.number() = 3;

steps.label: Find meetings that require follow-up using dot notation with boolean cast
steps.code:
SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY)
FROM client_interactions ci
WHERE ci.data.type.string() = 'meeting'
  AND ci.data.follow_up_required.boolean() = TRUE;

steps.label: Find all interactions for client 3 using dot notation with number cast

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM client_interactions ci WHERE ci.data.type.string() = 'meeting' AND ci.data.follow_up_required.boolean() = TRUE
exercises.checkpoint: You just filtered documents using Oracle's type-safe dot notation — .string(), .number(), .boolean(). No schema definition. No upfront DDL. The database resolved paths at query time.

exercises.title: Insert a Document
exercises.description: Insert a new interaction document and verify it was stored.
exercises.explanation:
The **JSON()** constructor is the write-time gateway between your application's text representation and Oracle's binary storage engine. When you pass a JSON string literal to the `JSON()` function, Oracle parses the text, validates its syntactic correctness, and converts it into **OSON binary** — the hash-indexed format that gives every subsequent read O(1) field access. If your JSON is malformed — missing a comma, unbalanced braces, trailing garbage — the constructor rejects it immediately with a parse error. This is strict validation at ingestion time, which prevents corrupt documents from ever entering the collection.

[svg:m1-json-constructor.svg]

The conversion to OSON is not a passive storage optimization. It's an active transformation that builds a field offset table, encodes type metadata for each value, and compresses repeated field names across the document. This means the first INSERT pays a small conversion cost, but every subsequent SELECT, WHERE filter, JSON_TRANSFORM update, and index lookup against that document benefits from direct field navigation. The economics are clear: you write once and read many times, so amortizing the conversion cost over reads is the correct engineering tradeoff.

The **COMMIT** after the INSERT is not optional in a multi-user environment. Until you commit, the new row exists only in your transaction's undo space — visible to your session but invisible to everyone else. This is Oracle's **read-consistent** isolation model, and it applies identically to JSON collections and relational tables. There's no separate consistency model for documents.

For alternatives to the text literal approach, **JSON_OBJECT()** builds a document from SQL expressions — useful when you're constructing documents from query results or bind variables. **JSON_ARRAY()** constructs array values programmatically. For bulk ingestion, external tables with JSON format or `INSERT ALL` with multiple `INTO` clauses outperform row-at-a-time inserts by orders of magnitude. The JSON constructor also handles Oracle-extended types like DATE and TIMESTAMP, converting them to ISO 8601 strings in the resulting document.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY) FROM client_interactions ci WHERE ci.data."date".string() >= '2026-03-30' ORDER BY ci.data."date".string() DESC FETCH FIRST 3 ROWS ONLY` — find the most recent interactions including the one you just inserted
- `INSERT INTO client_interactions (data) VALUES (JSON_OBJECT('client_id' VALUE 1001, 'type' VALUE 'note', 'summary' VALUE 'Built with JSON_OBJECT'))` — try building a document from key-value pairs instead of a literal
exercises.code:
-- Insert a new interaction
INSERT INTO client_interactions (data) VALUES (JSON('{
    "client_id": 3,
    "advisor_id": 5,
    "type": "email",
    "date": "2026-03-30T14:00:00Z",
    "channel": "email",
    "summary": "Follow-up on rebalancing discussion.",
    "action_items": [],
    "sentiment": "positive",
    "follow_up_required": false
}'));
COMMIT;

-- Verify it's there
SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY)
FROM client_interactions ci
WHERE ci.data.type.string() = 'email'
  AND ci.data.client_id.number() = 3;

steps.label: Insert a new interaction document using the JSON() constructor
steps.code:
INSERT INTO client_interactions (data) VALUES (JSON('{
    "client_id": 3,
    "advisor_id": 5,
    "type": "email",
    "date": "2026-03-30T14:00:00Z",
    "channel": "email",
    "summary": "Follow-up on rebalancing discussion.",
    "action_items": [],
    "sentiment": "positive",
    "follow_up_required": false
}'));
COMMIT

steps.label: Verify the new document exists
steps.code:
SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY)
FROM client_interactions ci
WHERE ci.data.type.string() = 'email'
  AND ci.data.client_id.number() = 3;

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM client_interactions ci WHERE ci.data.type.string() = 'email' AND ci.data.client_id.number() = 3
exercises.checkpoint: You just inserted a JSON document using the JSON() constructor. Oracle converted it to OSON binary on write. One INSERT, one table, zero schema changes.

exercises.title: Update with JSON_TRANSFORM
exercises.description: Add an action item to an existing interaction and change its sentiment using JSON_TRANSFORM.
exercises.explanation:
**JSON_TRANSFORM** is Oracle's answer to the read-modify-write anti-pattern that plagues document databases. In a typical application-level update, you fetch the document from the database, deserialize it into an object, mutate a field, serialize it back to JSON, and write the entire document back — a four-step round trip that introduces network latency, serialization overhead, and race conditions if two sessions modify the same document concurrently. JSON_TRANSFORM eliminates all four steps by performing the mutation **server-side**, directly against the OSON binary representation, within the database engine itself.

[svg:m1-json-transform.svg]

The operation vocabulary is precise. **SET** replaces a scalar value at a given path — if the path exists, the value is overwritten. **APPEND** adds an element to the end of an existing array. **REMOVE** deletes a field or array element entirely. **RENAME** changes a field's key name without touching its value. **INSERT** adds a new field only if it doesn't already exist (use **CREATE ON MISSING** with SET if you want upsert behavior). These operations compose: you can chain multiple SET, APPEND, and REMOVE operations in a single JSON_TRANSFORM call, and Oracle applies them atomically in a single pass over the document. No intermediate states are visible to other sessions.

The path expression syntax uses **JSONPath** notation. `$.action_items` navigates to the `action_items` field at the document root. `$.action_items[0].status` reaches into the first array element's `status` field. The **JSON()** wrapper around the APPEND value converts your text literal to OSON binary before insertion, maintaining type consistency within the array.

Error handling is configurable per operation. **IGNORE ON MISSING** silently skips an operation if the target path doesn't exist — useful for defensive updates against heterogeneous documents. **NULL ON MISSING** returns NULL. **ERROR ON MISSING** raises an exception, which is appropriate when the path must exist and its absence indicates a data integrity problem. These clauses give you the same level of control over edge cases that you'd expect from a mature relational UPDATE statement.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT json_serialize(json_transform(JSON('{"a":1,"b":[1,2]}'), SET '$.a' = 99, APPEND '$.b' = 3, INSERT '$.c' = 'new') PRETTY) FROM dual` — experiment with multiple operations in one call without touching real data
- `SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY) FROM client_interactions ci WHERE ci.data.client_id.number() = 3 AND ci.data.type.string() = 'meeting'` — verify your updates landed correctly

steps.label: Append a new action item to the meeting's action_items array
steps.code:
UPDATE client_interactions ci
SET ci.data = json_transform(ci.data,
    APPEND '$.action_items' = JSON('{"task":"Schedule Q2 review","due":"2026-04-15","status":"pending"}')
)
WHERE ci.data.client_id.number() = 3
  AND ci.data.type.string() = 'meeting';
COMMIT

steps.label: Change the sentiment field to 'optimistic' using SET
steps.code:
UPDATE client_interactions ci
SET ci.data = json_transform(ci.data,
    SET '$.sentiment' = 'optimistic'
)
WHERE ci.data.client_id.number() = 3
  AND ci.data.type.string() = 'meeting';
COMMIT

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM client_interactions ci WHERE ci.data.client_id.number() = 3 AND ci.data.type.string() = 'meeting' AND JSON_EXISTS(ci.data, '$.action_items[*]?(@.task == "Schedule Q2 review")')
exercises.checkpoint: You just modified documents in place with JSON_TRANSFORM — APPEND to add array elements, SET to change values. Server-side, atomic, no read-modify-write cycle.

exercises.title: Create a Search Index and Full-Text Query
exercises.description: Create a JSON search index and perform full-text search across document fields.
exercises.explanation:
**CREATE SEARCH INDEX ... FOR JSON** instructs Oracle to build an Oracle Text full-text index over every string field in every document in your collection. The **FOR JSON** clause is the critical differentiator — it tells the indexing engine to introspect the JSON structure of each document and index all string values at all paths automatically. You don't enumerate fields. You don't maintain a mapping. When a new document arrives with a field that has never existed before, that field is indexed on its next sync cycle without any DDL change. This is schema-aware indexing on schemaless data.

[svg:m1-search-index.svg]

The query function **JSON_TEXTCONTAINS** takes three arguments: the JSON column, a path expression, and the search term. The path `'$.summary'` restricts the search to the summary field. The path `'$'` searches across **all** string fields simultaneously — every field at every nesting level. The search term passes through Oracle Text's linguistic processing pipeline: tokenization, stemming (so `'rebalance'` matches `'rebalancing'` and `'rebalanced'`), stopword removal, and optionally fuzzy matching for misspellings.

The consistency model is where Oracle separates from every other document database with search capabilities. Oracle's search index is updated **within the same transaction** as the data modification. When you INSERT a document and COMMIT, the search index reflects that document immediately — not after a sync delay, not eventually. Compare this to MongoDB Atlas Search, which runs on a separate Lucene-based process with **1 to 15 seconds of eventual consistency lag**. During that window, a query against the search index can return stale results that contradict a direct document read. In financial services — where compliance queries must reflect the current committed state of the data — that lag window is not an inconvenience. It's a regulatory risk.

JSON_TEXTCONTAINS supports **Boolean operators**: `'rebalance AND risk'` requires both terms, `'portfolio OR holdings'` matches either, `'NOT retirement'` excludes. You can combine these with **SCORE()** to rank results by relevance, transforming your JSON collection into a full search engine with transactional guarantees that no standalone search product can match.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY) FROM client_interactions ci WHERE JSON_TEXTCONTAINS(ci.data, '$', 'risk')` — search across ALL fields in the document, not just summary (run this after creating the search index above)
- `SELECT SCORE(1) AS relevance, ci.data.summary.string() FROM client_interactions ci WHERE JSON_TEXTCONTAINS(ci.data, '$.summary', 'rebalance', 1) ORDER BY SCORE(1) DESC` — rank results by relevance score (run this after creating the search index above)
exercises.code:
-- Create a JSON search index
CREATE SEARCH INDEX idx_interactions_search
ON client_interactions (data) FOR JSON;

-- Full-text search across all document fields
SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY)
FROM client_interactions ci
WHERE JSON_TEXTCONTAINS(ci.data, '$.summary', 'rebalance');

steps.label: Create a full-text search index on the JSON data column
steps.code:
CREATE SEARCH INDEX idx_interactions_search
ON client_interactions (data) FOR JSON;

steps.label: Search for documents containing 'rebalance' in the summary field

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM user_indexes WHERE index_name = 'IDX_INTERACTIONS_SEARCH'
exercises.checkpoint: You just built a full-text search index on JSON documents and queried it with JSON_TEXTCONTAINS. Transactionally consistent — unlike MongoDB Atlas Search's 1-15 second eventual consistency lag.

exercises.title: Aggregate Documents with SQL
exercises.description: Count interactions by type and calculate average action items per interaction.
exercises.explanation:
This is where the convergence thesis pays its dividend. You're running **GROUP BY**, **COUNT**, and **AVG** — operators that have been the backbone of relational analytics for four decades — directly against schemaless JSON documents. No MapReduce pipeline. No `$group` aggregation stage with its own non-SQL syntax. No exporting documents to a data warehouse because your document database can't do analytics. Oracle's **Cost-Based Optimizer** looks at this query, evaluates the data statistics, and chooses the optimal execution plan — hash grouping for large cardinalities, sort grouping for small ones — using the same planning algorithms it applies to relational tables.

[svg:m1-sql-aggregation.svg]

The dot notation `ci.data.type.string()` extracts the grouping key as a VARCHAR2, and the optimizer treats it exactly like a column reference in a relational GROUP BY. The `json_value` function with the path `'$.action_items.size()'` computes the array length — **size()** is a built-in JSONPath function that returns the element count of an array without requiring you to unnest it. The **RETURNING NUMBER** clause converts the result to a SQL number so it can participate in arithmetic operations like AVG and SUM. Without RETURNING NUMBER, json_value defaults to returning a VARCHAR2, and `AVG('3')` is not what you want.

This composability is the key insight. Every SQL feature that works on relational columns — **HAVING** to filter groups after aggregation, **ORDER BY** on computed columns, **ROLLUP** and **CUBE** for multi-dimensional summaries, **window functions** like RANK() and LAG() — works identically on values extracted from JSON documents via dot notation or json_value. You aren't learning a parallel query language for documents. You're using the same SQL you already know, applied to a different storage format. The optimizer doesn't care whether the value came from a relational column or a JSON path expression. It sees a typed value and plans accordingly.

In MongoDB, this equivalent operation requires the aggregation framework — `db.collection.aggregate([{$group: {_id: "$type", total: {$sum: 1}}}])` — a pipeline syntax that's powerful but proprietary, not composable with standard SQL tooling, and invisible to the four decades of optimizer research that Oracle brings to every query.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT ci.data.sentiment.string() AS sentiment, COUNT(*) AS total FROM client_interactions ci GROUP BY ci.data.sentiment.string() ORDER BY total DESC` — pivot on sentiment instead of type to see mood distribution
- `SELECT ci.data.client_id.number() AS client, COUNT(*) AS interactions FROM client_interactions ci GROUP BY ci.data.client_id.number() HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 ORDER BY interactions DESC` — find the most active clients using HAVING to filter
exercises.code:
-- Count interactions by type
SELECT ci.data.type.string() AS interaction_type,
       COUNT(*) AS total
FROM client_interactions ci
GROUP BY ci.data.type.string()
ORDER BY total DESC;

-- Average action items per interaction
SELECT ci.data.type.string() AS interaction_type,
       AVG(json_value(ci.data, '$.action_items.size()' RETURNING NUMBER)) AS avg_actions
FROM client_interactions ci
GROUP BY ci.data.type.string();

steps.label: Count interactions grouped by type using dot notation extraction
steps.code:
SELECT ci.data.type.string() AS interaction_type,
       COUNT(*) AS total
FROM client_interactions ci
GROUP BY ci.data.type.string()
ORDER BY total DESC;

steps.label: Calculate average action items per interaction type using json_value
steps.code:
SELECT ci.data.type.string() AS interaction_type,
       AVG(json_value(ci.data, '$.action_items.size()' RETURNING NUMBER)) AS avg_actions
FROM client_interactions ci
GROUP BY ci.data.type.string();

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT ci.data.type.string()) AS type_count FROM client_interactions ci
exercises.checkpoint: You just ran SQL aggregations — GROUP BY, COUNT, AVG — directly on JSON documents. Same optimizer that's been optimizing relational queries for 40 years. Documents and SQL are not competing paradigms.
